Slide 7:
Undecidable Language
Consider polynomials with one variable (e.g., 2x2+ x −7)
Let
D1 = {⟨p⟩| ⟨p⟩is a polynomial over x with an integral root}
Is D1 decidable?
Yes, if there exists a Turing machine that accepts every
⟨p⟩∈D1 and rejects every ⟨p⟩̸∈D1
Luckily there is an upper/lower bound of the value of x that a
machine needs to test:
±kcmax
c1
where k is the number of terms in the polynomial, cmax is the
coeﬃcient with the largest absolute value, and c1 is the
coeﬃcient of the highest order term
Change M1 such that it rejects after testing value goes
out-of-bound
Therefore, D1 is decidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Turing Machine 04

Slide 8:
Undecidable Language
Let D be the set of all polynomials that have integral root
D = {⟨p⟩| ⟨p⟩is a polynomial with an integral root}
We can create a machine that tries all possible assignment
values starting from 0s
For example, in case of two variables x and y, try the following
values [x, y]:
[0, 0], [0, 1], [1, 0], [1, 1], [0, −1], [−1, 0], [−1, −1], [0, 2], . . .
If a polynomial p has an integral root, eventually it will be
evaluated to 0
Therefore, D is recognizable
Unfortunately, there is no bound that we can check and
machine may loop inﬁnitely
If the polynomial p does not have an integral root, we will keep
trying new values of [x, y] forever (loop indeﬁnitely)
D is not decidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Turing Machine 04

Slide 10:
Describing Turing Machines
An implementation description of the previous Turing
machine that decides {w#w | w ∈{0, 1}∗} is shown below
On input string w:
1
Zig-zag across the tape to corresponding positions on either
side of the # symbol to check whether those positions contain
the same symbol. If they do not, or if no # is found, reject.
Cross oﬀsymbols as they are checked to keep track of which
symbols correspond.
2
When all symbols to the left of the # have been crossed oﬀ,
check for any remaining symbols to the right of the #. If any
symbols remain, reject; otherwise, accept.
Note that the above description describes the way the Turing
machine moves its head and store data (cross oﬀsymbols)
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Turing Machine 04

Slide 11:
Describing Turing Machines
An high-level description of the previous Turing machine
that decides {w#w | w ∈{0, 1}∗} is shown below:
M =“On input s where s = x#y for some string x and y:
1
Compare whether the string x is identical to the string y.
2
If they are identical, accept; otherwise, reject.”
Note that the where clause behaves like a ﬁlter
Any string that does not satisfy the where clause will be
rejected immediately
What a TM can do?
From the Church-Turing thesis, if there is an algorithm to do
something, a TM can do the same thing
Examples:
Compare two strings
Check whether the length of a string is a power of 2
Addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, modulo
Any algorithms discussed in Chapter 1
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

The following machine M′decides A using TM M as a

1
For every i where 1 ≤i ≤n −1:
2
Run M on input xi#xi+1.
3
If M rejects, reject.
4
accept”

Algorithm and Turing Machine are consider equivalent
Anything that an algorithm can do, there exists a TM that can
do the same thing

Slide 2:
Decidability
A language is decidable if there is a decider decides it
A language is a set of strings
A decider is a Turing machine that halts on all inputs
A Turing machine halts when it enters either the accept state
(qaccept) or the reject state (qreject)
To show that a language A is decidable:
Construct a TM M that you think it decides the language A
and prove that
1
For every s ∈A, M must accept s and
2
For every s ̸∈A, M must reject s
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

This allows us to conclude the following:
1
L(M) = A
M accepts all strings in A and does not accept all strings not
in A
2
M is a decider (always halts)
Given a string s, it is either in A or not in A
If s ∈A, M accepts s (halts)
If s ̸∈A, M rejects s (halts)
3
A is decidable
The decider M decides A

The problem of determining whether a DFA accepts a string.
Let ⟨x⟩be a string representation of the object x
The above problem can be converted into a membership
problem (language) ADFA
ADFA = {⟨B, w⟩| B is a DFA that accepts input string w}
Now, the problem whether DFA B accepts the string w
becomes the problem whether ⟨B, w⟩is in ADFA

2
If the simulation ends in an accept state, accept. If it ends in a

Slide 9:
Is M a Decider for the Language ADFA
Again, to show that the Turing machine M is a decider for
the language ADFA, we have to show that
1
If ⟨B, w⟩∈ADFA, M accepts w
2
If ⟨B, w⟩̸∈ADFA, M rejects w
Proofs:
Case 1: Assume that ⟨B, w⟩∈ADFA. Since ⟨B, w⟩∈ADFA,
by the deﬁnition of ADFA, B is a DFA that accepts the string
w. Since B accepts w, by simulating B on input w in step 1,
the simulation will end in an accept state. Since the simulation
ends in an accept state, M accepts ⟨B, w⟩.
Case 2: Assume that ⟨B, w⟩̸∈ADFA. Since ⟨B, w⟩̸∈ADFA,
by the deﬁnition of ADFA, B is a DFA that rejects the string
w. Since B rejects w, by simulating B on input w in step 1,
the simulation will end in a non-accept state. Since the
simulation ends in a non-accept state, M rejects ⟨B, w⟩.
We just show that M accepts all strings in ADFA and rejects
all strings not in ADFA
M is a decider for ADFA. Therefore, ADFA is decidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 01

Slide 10:
Prove that ADFA is Decidable
Prove that
ADFA = {⟨B, w⟩| B is a DFA that accepts w}
is decidable.
Solution: Construct a TM M as follows:
M =“On input ⟨B, w⟩, where B is a DFA and w is a string:
1
Simulate B on input w.
2
If the simulation ends in an accept state, accept. If it ends in a nonaccepting
state, reject.”
Next, we need to prove that M decides ADFA:
Case 1: Assume that ⟨B, w⟩∈ADFA. Since ⟨B, w⟩∈ADFA, by the deﬁnition
of ADFA, B is a DFA that accepts the string w. Since B accepts w, by
simulating B on input w in step 1, the simulation will end in an accept state.
Since the simulation ends in an accept state, M accepts ⟨B, w⟩.
Case 2: Assume that ⟨B, w⟩̸∈ADFA. Since ⟨B, w⟩̸∈ADFA, by the deﬁnition
of ADFA, B is a DFA that rejects the string w. Since B rejects w, by
simulating B on input w in step 1, the simulation will end in a non-accept state.
Since the simulation ends in a non-accept state, M rejects ⟨B, w⟩.
This shows that M is a decider for ADFA. Therefore, ADFA is decidable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

ANFA
Let ANFA = {⟨B, w⟩| B is an NFA that accepts input string w}.
Show that ANFA is decidable.
Again, to show that ANFA is decidable, we need to construct
a TM N and prove the following:
1
If ⟨B, w⟩∈ANFA, N accepts ⟨B, w⟩
2
If ⟨B, w⟩̸∈ANFA, N rejects ⟨B, w⟩
As discussed earlier in Chapter 1, there is an algorithm that
allows us convert an NFA B into an equivalent DFA C
Since there is an algorithm, a Turing machine that convert an
NFA B into a equivalent DFA C where L(B) = L(C)
If NFA B accepts w, DFA C also accepts w
If DFA C accepts w, ⟨C, w⟩∈ADFA
If ⟨C, w⟩∈ADFA, TM M (decider for ADFA) accept ⟨C, w⟩

for this conversion given in Theorem 1.39.
2
Run TM M from Theorem 4.1 on input ⟨C, w⟩.

C is a DFA that it just constructed from NFA B

Slide 13:
Prove that ANFA is Decidable
Prove that ANFA = {⟨B, w⟩| B is an NFA that accepts w} is decidable.
Solution: Construct a TM N as follows:
N =“On input ⟨B, w⟩, where B is a NFA and w is a string:
1
Convert NFA B to an equivalent DFA C, using the procedure for this conversion
given in Theorem 1.39.
2
Run TM M from Theorem 4.1 on input ⟨C, w⟩.
3
If M accepts ⟨C, w⟩, accept; otherwise, reject.”
Next, we need to prove that N decides ANFA:
1
Case 1: Assume that ⟨B, w⟩∈ANFA. Since ⟨B, w⟩∈ANFA, by the deﬁnition
of the language ANFA, B is an NFA that accepts the string w. By converting
the NFA B into an equivalent DFA C (L(B) = L(C)), C also accepts the string
w. Since C is a DFA that accepts the string w, ⟨C, w⟩∈ADFA. Since M is a
decider for ADFA, by running M on input ⟨C, w⟩, M will accept ⟨C, w⟩which
will cause N to accept ⟨B, w⟩.
2
Case 2: Assume that ⟨B, w⟩̸∈ANFA, Since ⟨B, w⟩̸∈ANFA, by the deﬁnition
of the language ANFA, B is an NFA that rejects the string w. By converting
the NFA B into an equivalent DFA C (L(B) = L(C)), C also rejects the string
w. Since C is a DFA that rejects the string w, ⟨C, w⟩̸∈ADFA. Since M is a
decider for ADFA, by running M on input ⟨C, w⟩, M will reject ⟨C, w⟩which
will cause N to reject ⟨B, w⟩.
This shows that TM N is a decider for ANFA. Therefore, ANFA is decidable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 15:
Prove that AREX is Decidable
Prove that AREX = {⟨R, w⟩| R is a regular expression that generates string w} is
decidable.
Solution: Construct a TM P as follows:
P =“On input ⟨R, w⟩, where R is a regular express and w is a string:
1
Convert regular expression R to an equivalent NFA A by using the procedure for
this conversion given in Theorem 1.54.
2
Run TM N from Theorem 4.2 on input ⟨A, w⟩.
3
If N accepts ⟨A, w⟩, accept; if N rejects ⟨A, w⟩, reject.”
Next, we need to prove that P decides AREX:
1
Case 1: Assume that ⟨R, w⟩∈AREX. Since ⟨R, w⟩∈AREX, from the
deﬁnition of AREX, R is a regular expression that generates the string w. Since
R is a regular expression that generates w, by converting R into an equivalent
NFA A, A accepts w. Since NFA A accepts w, ⟨A, w⟩∈ANFA. Since TM N is
a decider for ANFA, by running N on input ⟨A, w⟩, N will accept ⟨A, w⟩which
causes P to accept ⟨R, w⟩.
2
Case 2: Assume that ⟨R, w⟩̸∈AREX. Since ⟨R, w⟩̸∈AREX, from the
deﬁnition of AREX, R is a regular expression that does not generate the string
w. Since R is a regular expression that does not generate w, by converting R
into an equivalent NFA A, A rejects w. Since NFA A rejects w,
⟨A, w⟩̸∈ANFA. Since TM N is a decider for ANFA, by running N on input
⟨A, w⟩, N will reject ⟨A, w⟩which causes P to reject ⟨R, w⟩.
This shows that TM P is a decider for AREX. Therefore, AREX is decidable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 5:
Decidable Languages
EQDFA
Let EQDFA = {⟨A, B⟩| A and B are DFAs and L(A) = L(B)}.
Show that EQDFA is decidable.
Given two DFAs A and B, is there any algorithm to check
whether L(A) = L(B)?
In other words, can we have a Turing machine F such that
If ⟨A, B⟩∈EQDFA (L(A) = L(B)), F will accept ⟨A, B⟩and
If ⟨A, B⟩̸∈EQDFA (L(A) ̸= L(B)), F will reject ⟨A, B⟩
Again, we cannot simply run both DFAs on all strings until
one accept but the other reject
This may loop indeﬁnitely
Since L(A) and L(B) are sets, we need to use the set theory
L(A) = L(B) iﬀL(A) ⊆L(B) and L(B) ⊆L(A)

Slide 7:
The set of regular languages
Closed Under Complement:
Given a DFA A = (Q, Σ, δ, q0, F) we can construct a DFA A′
where L(A′) = L(A).
A′= (Q, Σ, δ, q0, Q −F)
Closed Under Union:
Given two DFAs A = (QA, Σ, δA, qA, FA) and
B = (QB, Σ, δB, qB, FB) we can construct a DFA
C = (QC, Σ, δC, qC, FC) where L(C) = L(A) ∪L(B).
QC = QA × QB
δC((qi, qj), x) = (δA(qi, x), δB(qj, x)) where qi ∈QA and
qj ∈QB.
qC = (qA, qB)
FC = {(qi, qj) | qi ∈FA or qj ∈FB}.
Closed Under Intersection:
Same as Closed Under Union except that
FC = {(qi, qj) | qi ∈FA and qj ∈FB}.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 02

Slide 8:
Decidable Language
To show that
(L(A) ∩L(B)) ∪(L(A) ∩L(B)) = ∅
We need to construct a DFA C such that
L(C) = (L(A) ∩L(B)) ∪(L(A) ∩L(B))
and check that L(C) = ∅(⟨C⟩∈EDFA)
This can be done by:
1
Construct DFA D such that L(D) = L(B)
2
Construct DFA E such that L(E) = L(A) ∩L(D)
3
Construct DFA F such that L(F) = L(A)
4
Construct DFA G such that L(G) = L(F) ∩L(B)
5
Construct DFA C such that L(C) = L(E) ∪L(G)
6
Run T (a decider for EDFA) on input ⟨C⟩

Decidable Languages
For simplicity, we can simply construct a DFA C such that
L(C) = (L(A) ∩L(B)) ∪(L(A) ∩L(B))
Again, we need to show that C accepts no string (L(C) = ∅)
This is an example of a TM:
F =“On input ⟨A, B⟩, where A and B are DFAs:
1
Construct DFA C such that
L(C) = (L(A) ∩L(B)) ∪(L(A) ∩L(B))
2
Run TM T from Theorem 4.4 on input ⟨C⟩.
3
If T accepts ⟨C⟩, accept. If T rejects ⟨C⟩, reject.

Slide 10:
Prove that EQDFA is Decidable
Prove that EQDFA = {⟨A, B⟩| A and B are DFAs and L(A) = L(B)} is decidable.
Solution: Construct a TM F as follows:
F =“On input ⟨A, B⟩, where A and B are DFAs:
1
Construct DFA C such that L(C) = (L(A) ∩L(B)) ∪(L(A) ∩L(B))
2
Run TM T from Theorem 4.4 on input ⟨C⟩.
3
If T accepts ⟨C⟩, accept. If T rejects ⟨C⟩, reject.
Now, we need to prove that F is a decider for EQDFA.
1
Assume that ⟨A, B⟩∈EQDFA. Since ⟨A, B⟩∈EQDFA, A and B are DFAs
and L(A) = L(B). Since L(A) = L(B), according to the deﬁnition of the
language of DFA C, L(C) = ∅. Since L(C) = ∅, ⟨C⟩∈EDFA. By running T
on input ⟨C⟩, T will accept ⟨C⟩. Since T accepts ⟨C⟩, F accepts ⟨A, B⟩.
2
Assume that ⟨A, B⟩̸∈EQDFA. Since ⟨A, B⟩̸∈EQDFA, A and B are DFAs
and L(A) ̸= L(B). Since L(A) ̸= L(B), according to the deﬁnition of the
language of DFA C, L(C) ̸= ∅. Since L(C) ̸= ∅, ⟨C⟩̸∈EDFA. By running T
on input ⟨C⟩, T will reject ⟨C⟩. Since T rejects ⟨C⟩, F rejects ⟨A, B⟩.
This shows that F is a decider for EQDFA. Therefore, EQDFA is decidable.

input:
Slide 11:
Decidability
So far we have the following Turing machines:
1
TM M that decides ADFA where
ADFA = {⟨B, w⟩| B is a DFA that accepts w}
2
TM N that decides ANFA where
ANFA = {⟨B, w⟩| B is an NFA that accepts w}
3
TM P that decides AREX
AREX = {⟨R, w⟩| R is a regular expression that generates w}
4
TM T that decides EDFA
EDFA = {⟨A⟩| A is an DFA and L(A) = ∅}
5
TM F that decides EQDFA
EQDFA = {⟨A, B⟩| A and B are DFAs and L(A) = L(B)}
We can use these TMs as helper machines
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 02

Slide 12:
ONE DFA
How do we know that a DFA accepts exactly one string?
This problem corresponds with the language ONE DFA as
follows:
ONE DFA = {⟨A⟩| A is a DFA and |L(A)| = 1}
How to show that ONE DFA is decidable?
Construct a TM M′such that
1
If ⟨A⟩∈ONE DFA, M′accepts ⟨A⟩
2
If ⟨A⟩̸∈ONE DFA, M′rejects ⟨A⟩
Note that we cannot run a DFA A on all string until it
accepts a string because it may loop indeﬁnitely
But if L(A) ̸= ∅, it will not loop indeﬁnitely
So, we need to check that the language of a given DFA is not
empty ﬁrst
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

input:
Slide 13:
ONE DFA
Construct a TM M′as follows:
M′= “On input ⟨A⟩where A is a DFA:
1
Run T on input ⟨A⟩
2
If T accepts ⟨A⟩, reject.
3
If T rejects ⟨A⟩, run A on all inputs until it accepts an input
string w
4
Construct a DFA B such that L(B) = {w}
5
Run F on input ⟨A, B⟩.
6
If F accepts ⟨A, B⟩, accept. Otherwise, reject.”
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 02

Slide 14:
ONE DFA
Prove that if ⟨A⟩∈ONE DFA, M′accepts ⟨A⟩:
Assume that ⟨A⟩∈ONE DFA. Since ⟨A⟩∈ONE DFA, A is a DFA and
|L(A)| = 1. Since |L(A)| = 1, L(A) ̸= ∅. Since L(A) ̸= ∅, ⟨A⟩̸∈EDFA. Since
T is a decider for EDFA, by running T on input ⟨A⟩, T will reject ⟨A⟩. Since
|L(A)| = 1, by running A on all strings, it will eventually accept a string w.
Since |L(A)| = 1 and A accepts w, L(A) = {w} which is the same as the
language of B. Since L(A) = L(B), ⟨A, B⟩∈EQDFA. By running F on input
⟨A, B⟩, F will accept ⟨A, B⟩. Since F accepts ⟨A, B⟩, M′accepts ⟨A⟩.
Prove that if ⟨A⟩̸∈ONE DFA, M′rejects ⟨A⟩:
Assume that ⟨A⟩̸∈ONE DFA. Since ⟨A⟩̸∈ONE DFA, A is a DFA and
|L(A)| ̸= 1. In other words, L(A) = ∅or |L(A)| > 1.
If L(A) = ∅, ⟨A⟩∈EDFA. Since T is a decider for EDFA, by running T on
input ⟨A⟩, T will accept ⟨A⟩. Since T accepts ⟨A⟩, M′rejects ⟨A⟩.
If |L(A)| > 1, by running A on all strings, it will eventually accept a string w.
Since |L(A)| > 1 and A accepts w, L(A) ̸= {w} which is not the same as the
language of B. Since L(A) ̸= L(B), ⟨A, B⟩̸∈EQDFA. By running F on input
⟨A, B⟩, F will reject ⟨A, B⟩. Since F rejects ⟨A, B⟩, M′rejects ⟨A⟩.
This shows that M′is a decider for ONE DFA. Therefore, ONE DFA is decidable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 2:
Undecidability
Consider the following language:
ATM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM and M accepts w}
Is ATM recognizable?
To show that ATM is recognizable, you need to construct a
TM that accepts all strings in ATM
But if a string is not in ATM, no need to explicitly reject
Loop indeﬁnitely is acceptable
The following is an example of a TM that recognizes ATM:
U =“On input ⟨M, w⟩, where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Simulate M on input w.
2
If M ever enters its accept state, accept; if M enters its reject
state, reject.”
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 3:
Undecidability
ATM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM and M accepts w}
The following is an example of a TM that recognizes ATM:
U =“On input ⟨M, w⟩, where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Simulate M on input w.
2
If M ever enters its accept state, accept; if M enters its reject
state, reject.”
Let’s look at the above machine:
If ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM:
M is a TM that accepts w
TM U will accept ⟨M, w⟩
If ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM:
1
M is a TM that rejects w
TM U will reject ⟨M, w⟩
2
M is a TM that loops indeﬁnitely on input w
TM U will loop indeﬁnitely on input ⟨M, w⟩
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 03

Slide 4:
Undecidability
TM U accepts all strings in ATM and does not accept
(either rejects or loop indeﬁnitely) on all strings not in ATM
ATM is recognizable
Is ATM decidable?
Unfortunately, ATM is undecidable
But how to prove it?
We will try to prove that it is undecidable later
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 5:
Unrecognizable Lanugage
Corollary 4.18
Some languages are not Turing-recognizable.
To show that there exists an unrecognizable language, we only
need to ﬁnd a language that is not recognized by any Turing
machines
It is not not easy to come up with such a language
Note that a Turing machine recognizes only one language
To prove that an unrecognizable language exists, we can
simply show that the number of languages is more than
the number of Turing machines
But we have a problem
There are inﬁnite number of languages
There are inﬁnite number of Turing machines
How do we know which set is larger?
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 03

Slide 6:
Cantor’s Observation
Cantor observed that two ﬁnite sets A and B have the same
size if we can pair one element from A with the element of B
{a, s, d, f}
{a, s, d, f}
{1, 4, 7, 3}
{1, 4, 7, 3}
Pairing #1
Pairing #2
The pairing does not have to be unique
We can consider pairing method as a function f : A →B that
has the following properties:
one-to-one: f(a) ̸= f(b) whenever a ̸= b
onto: For every b ∈B there is an a ∈A such that f(a) = b
Functions that are one-to-one and onto are called bijection or
correspondence
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

between N and E
A mapping function can be f : N →E where f(n) = 2n

Slide 9:
Countable
Deﬁnition 4.14
A set A is countable if either it is ﬁnite or it has the same size as
N.
To show that an inﬁnite set A is countable, we need to show
that it has the same size as N
To show that an inﬁnite set A has the same size as N, we
need to show a correspondence f : N →A between A and N
Can be a deﬁnition of a function (one-to-one and onto)
Can be a table that demonstrates one-to-one and onto
An inﬁnite set B is uncountable if there is no correspondence
with N
An inﬁnite set A is smaller than an inﬁnite set B if A is
countable but B is not countable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 2:
The Set of Real Numbers is Uncountable
A real number R is a number with decimal representation
π = 3.14159265 . . .
345 = 345.000 . . .
1.23 = 1.23000 . . .
To show that a set R is not countable, we generally use proof
by contradiction
Assume that R is countable
R is countable, there is a correspondence between N and R
Since there is a correspondence between N and R, there are
inﬁnitely many correspondence between N and R
This allows us to ﬁll the right column of the correspondence
table any way we want (no duplicate):
n ∈N
f(n) ∈R
1
?
2
?
3
?

Slide 13:
The Set of Turing Machines in Countable
Each Turing machine has its own encoding ⟨M⟩
A string representation of TM M
A string is a ﬁnite sequence of symbols over a Σ
⟨M⟩for every machine has a ﬁnite length
Similar to Σ∗, we can list all Turing machines in their
encoding form starting from the shortest length
Therefore, the set of Turing Machines is countable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 04

Slide 14:
Set of All Languages is Uncountable
Diagonalization works well when all elements are inﬁnite
We need to pick the nthof something
The nthdigit after the decimal point
The nthbit (from left)
If all elements are inﬁnite long, it is guaranteed to have the nth
of something
But some languages are ﬁnite and some are inﬁnite
Diagonalization does not work well for proving that the set of
all languages is uncountable

Same Size, Countable, and Uncountable
The following are what we have so far:
The set of all Turing machine is countable
The set of all inﬁnite binary sequences is uncountable
We know that an uncountable set is larger than a countable
set
We need to show that the set of all languages has the same
size as the set of all inﬁnite binary sequences
To show that two sets have the same size, we need to show a
correspondence
We need a correspondence between the set of all languages
and the set of all inﬁnite binary sequences

{ε, 0, 1, 00, 01, 10, 11, 000, 001, . . . }
Given a language (either ﬁnite or inﬁnite), we can also list the
language starting from the shortest string
The set of all strings that start with a 1
{1, 10, 11, 100, 101, 110, 111, 1000, . . . }
The set of all strings of length 2
{00, 01, 10, 11}
Note that we use dictionary order for those with the same
length

Slide 2:
An Undecidable Language
Theorem 4.11
Let ATM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM and M accepts w}. ATM is an
undecidable language.
Assume that ATM is decidable
Thus, there exists a Turing machine (decider) H that decides
ATM
Given a TM M and a string w in the form ⟨M, w⟩, H can
decide whether ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM or not
Let H(⟨M, w⟩) represents running TM H on input ⟨M, w⟩
Since H is a decider for ATM, we have
H(⟨M, w⟩) =
(
accept
if ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ⇝M accepts w
reject
if ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM ⇝M does not accept w
where M is a TM and w is a string
Note that H always halts since it is a decider
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 3:
An Undecidable Language
Note that an encoding of a machine M denoted by ⟨M⟩is
simply a string
Therefore, we can run M on input ⟨M⟩if we want to
Suppose we want to know whether M accepts ⟨M⟩
We cannot simply run M on input ⟨M⟩
M may loop indeﬁnitely on input ⟨M⟩and we may not know
But now we have TM H (a decider for ATM
Simply run H on input ⟨M, ⟨M⟩⟩
If H accepts ⟨M, ⟨M⟩⟩, M accepts ⟨M⟩
If H rejects ⟨M, ⟨M⟩⟩, M does not accept ⟨M⟩
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 05

Slide 4:
An Undecidable Language
Now, create a machine D as follows:
D = “On input ⟨M⟩, where M is a TM:
1
Run H on input ⟨M, ⟨M⟩⟩.
2
Output the opposite of what H outputs. That is, if H accepts,
reject; and if H rejects, accept.”
That is
D(⟨M⟩) =
(
accept
if H(⟨M, ⟨M⟩⟩) = reject ⇝M does not accept ⟨M⟩
reject
if H(⟨M, ⟨M⟩⟩) = accept ⇝M accepts ⟨M⟩
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 5:
An Undecidable Language
Note that an encoding of a machine D denoted by ⟨D⟩is also
a string
Therefore, we can run D on input ⟨D⟩
Let’s substitute M by D in the deﬁnite of TM D on the
previous slide
D(⟨D⟩) =
(
accept
if H(⟨D, ⟨D⟩⟩) = reject ⇝D does not accept ⟨D⟩
reject
if H(⟨D, ⟨D⟩⟩) = accept ⇝D accepts ⟨D⟩
The above equation says the following:
TM D accepts ⟨D⟩if D does not accept ⟨D⟩
TM D rejects ⟨D⟩if D accepts ⟨D⟩
which is contradict to itself
Therefore, ATM is undecidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 05

Slide 6:
Unrecognizable Language
Consider a recognizable language A
Since A is recognizable, there exists a machine MA that
accepts every string s ∈A
If s ̸∈A, MA may reject or loop indeﬁnitely on input s
Consider a recognizable language A (the complement of A)
Since A is recognizable, there exists a machine MA that
accepts every string s ∈A
If s ̸∈A, MA may reject or loop indeﬁnitely on input s
Both A and A are recognizable, is A decidable?
To show that A is decidable, we have to perform the following:
Construct a TM M′that we think it is a decider for A
Show that M′accepts all strings in A
Show that M′rejects all strings not in A
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 7:
Unrecognizable Language
The following is an example of the TM M′:
M′= “On input s:
1
Run both MA and MA on input s in parallel.
2
If MA accepts s, accept; if MA accepts s, reject.”
Case 1: Assume that s ∈A. Since s ∈A and MA recognizes
A, by running MA on input s, MA will accept s. Since s ̸∈A
and MA recognizes A, by running MA on input s, MA will
not accept s. Since MA accepts s, M′accepts s.
Case 2: Assume that s ̸∈A. Since s ̸∈A and MA recognizes
A, by running MA on input s, MA will not accept s. Since
s ∈A and MA recognizes A, by running MA on input s, MA
will accept s. Since MA accepts s, M′rejects s.
This show that M′is a decider for A
Therefore, A is decidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 05

Slide 8:
Unrecognizable Language
Now we just prove the following statement:
If a language A is recognizable and its complement A is
recognizable, A is decidable
Let
P ≡A is recognizable
Q ≡A is recognizable
R ≡A is decidable
The above statement is simply (P ∧Q) →R
From boolean algebra:
(P ∧Q) →R ≡¬(P ∧Q) ∨R
≡(¬P ∨¬Q) ∨R
≡(¬P ∨R) ∨¬Q
≡¬(P ∧¬R) ∨¬Q
≡(P ∧¬R) →¬Q
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 9:
Unrecognizable Language
From previous slide, we have
(P ∧Q) →R ≡(P ∧¬R) →¬Q
By replacing P, Q, and R on the right side, we have the
following statement:
If A is recognizable and A is undecidable, A is unrecognizable
Recall the following:
ATM is recognizable
ATM is undecidable
From the above statement, ATM is unrecognizable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 05

Slide 10:
Conclusions
To show that a language A is decidable:
Construct a TM M′that you think will decide A
Show that M′accepts all strings in A
Show that M′rejects all strings not in A
The following TMs can be used as helper machines:
TM M that decides ADFA
ADFA = {⟨B, w⟩| B is a DFA that accepts w}
TM N that decides ANFA
ANFA = {⟨B, w⟩| B is a NFA that accepts w}
TM P that decides AREX
AREX = {⟨R, w⟩| R is a regular expression that generates w}
TM T that decides EDFA
EDFA = {⟨B⟩| B is a DFA and L(B) = ∅}
TM F that decides EQDFA
EQDFA = {⟨A, B⟩| A and B are DFAs and L(A) = L(B)}
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Conclusions
To show that two sets have the same size, simply show a
correspondence between two sets
A correspondence must be one-to-one and onto
Can be a deﬁnition of a function
Can be represented by a table (skip duplicate)
To show that an inﬁnite set is countable, simply show a
correspondence with the set of natural number N
To show that an inﬁnite set is uncountable:
Diagonalization: if all elements in the set is somehow inﬁnite
Show a correspondence with a known uncountable set
ATM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM that accepts w} is undecidable
Given a TM M and a string w, there is no algorithm that can
check whether M accepts w
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Reducibility 01

Slide 2:
Reducibility
Reducibility is a way to convert a problem A into another
problem B where
a solution to the problem B can be used to solve problem A
Problems in our discussion are membership problems
Consider two sets A ⊆X and B ⊆Y and a function
f : X →Y such that
w ∈A ↔f(w) ∈B
Recall that
P ↔Q ≡(P →Q) ∧(Q →P)
= (¬P ∨Q) ∧(¬Q ∨P)
= (Q ∨¬P) ∧(P ∨¬Q)
= (¬Q →¬P) ∧(¬P →¬Q)
If the above property holds, we have:
If w ∈A, f(w) ∈B ⇝If f(w) ̸∈B, w ̸∈A
If f(w) ∈B, w ∈A ⇝If w ̸∈A, f(w) ̸∈B
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 3:
Reducibility
Again, consider two sets A ⊆X and B ⊆Y and a function
f : X →Y with the following property:
w ∈A ↔f(w) ∈B
Problem #1: Is a speciﬁc element in the set A
Problem #2: Is a speciﬁc element in the set B
Reducibility:
Given an element w ∈X, suppose no idea whether w ∈A
However, given an element s ∈Y, we know whether s ∈B
Suppose f() exists and the above property hold
Convert w to f(w) and check whether f(w) ∈B
If f(w) ∈B, w ∈A
If f(w) ̸∈B, w ̸∈A
Solution to problem #2 can be used to solve problem #1
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 01

Slide 4:
Example
Suppose we have a sport car and we need to know whether it
can go over 200 MPH
Unfortunately, we cannot drive our car that fast in Pittsburgh
Way too many potholes
Luckily, we have a psychic lady who has a very special ability
She can tell whether an object can explode into multiple pieces
So, let’s put a bomb into our sport car
Put the trigger at the speedometer at 200 MPH mark
Take the car to the psychic lady:
If she says that it can explode ⇝our sport car can go over 200
MPH
If she says that it cannot explode ⇝our sport car cannot go
over 200 MPH
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 9:
Reducibility
If B is decidable and f : Σ∗→Σ∗exists such that
x ∈A ↔f(x) ∈B
A is decidable.
Again, let’s apply some boolean algebra:
P ≡B is decidable
Q ≡f : Σ∗→Σ∗exists such that . . .
R ≡A is decidable
According to the boolean algebra, we have
(P ∧Q) →R ≡(¬R ∧Q) →¬P
If A is undecidable and f : Σ∗→Σ∗exists such that
x ∈A ↔f(x) ∈B
B is undecidable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 01

Slide 10:
Undecidability
How to prove that a language A is undecidable?
Use the fact that ATM is undecidable
But how?
Imagine if you can prove that
If A is decidable, ATM is decidable
The above statement is in the form of P →Q
Since P →Q ≡¬Q →¬P, we have
If ATM is undecidable, A is undecidable
This allows you to conclude that A is undecidable because
ATM is undecidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 11:
Use ATM
To show that a language A is undecidable (proof by
contradiction)
1
Assume that A is decidable
2
Show that if A is decidable, ATM is also decidable
3
Since ATM is decidable — contradiction
4
Therefore, A is undecidable
But why all the sudden ATM is decidable?
By assuming that a language A is decidable, it means there
exists a TM R that decides A
R is a decider for A
L(R) = A
R always halts
TM R can tell you whether a speciﬁc element is in A or not
If w ∈A, R accepts w
If w ̸∈A, R rejects w
This TM R will be our helper to help us decide ATM
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

We have no idea

We have no idea

We have no idea
4

We have no idea
What do we know about the given TM M and a string w?
Technically, we know almost nothing about M and w
But we know that if we run TM M on input w, there are three
possible outcomes:
1
M accepts w
2
M rejects w

M loops indeﬁnitely on w

Turing Machines as Methods

Slide 2:
Language of a Turing Machine
The language of a Turing machine M denoted by L(M) is the
set of all strings accepted by the Turing machine
Given a Turing machine M and a string w
If M accepts w, w ∈L(M)
If M rejects w, w ̸∈L(M)
If M loops indeﬁnitely on w, w ̸∈L(M)
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Turing Machines as Methods

Slide 3:
Language of a Method
Lets think about Turing machines as Java methods with the
following signature:
boolean method_name(String x)
Lets
true in Java means accept
false in Java means reject
Now, we can deﬁne the language of a method as the set of
all strings that the method returns true
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Turing Machines as Methods

Slide 4:
Language of a Method
Consider the following method
boolean M1(String x)
{
return true;
}
What is the language of the above method?
Obviously, M1() returns true no matter what argument of
type String you use
Thus, the language of the method M1() is Σ∗
The above method is pretty much the same as the Turing
machine below:
M1 = “On input x:
1
accept”
Note that L(M1) = Σ∗
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Turing Machines as Methods

Consider the following method
boolean M3(String x)
{
if(x.equals(""))
return true;
else
return false;
}
What is the language of the above method?
The above method only returns true if we call use the empty
string as the argument
For any other arguments that are not the empty string, it will
always return false
Thus, the language of the method M3() is {ε}

Slide 8:
Language of a Method
Suppose we have the method named is 0n1n with the
following signature:
boolean is_0n1n(String s)
This method returns true if the string s is in the form of 0n1n
for any n ≥0
It returns false for any other strings
Examples:
is 0n1n("") is true
is 0n1n("0101") is false
is 0n1n("01") is true
is 0n1n("00111") is false
is 0n1n("000111") is true

Thus, the language of the method M4() is {0n1n| n ≥0}

2
If x is not in the form of 0n1n, reject

Slide 11:
Language of a Method
Consider the following method
boolean M5(String x)
{
while(true);
return true;
}
What is the language of the above method?
Obviously, no matter what argument of type String you use
when call the above method, it will not return
It loops indeﬁnitely on all arguments.
Thus, the language of the method M5() is ∅
The above method M5() is pretty much the same as the
Turing machine below:
M5 = “On input x:
1
Enter inﬁnite loop
2
accept”
Note that L(M5) = ∅
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Turing Machines as Methods

Slide 12:
Language of a Method
Now suppose we have a method named M() and and a string
named w as follows:
boolean M(String s) {...}
String w = "...";
Note that we have no idea what is the method M()
It just a method that takes an argument of type String and
returns boolean
Just imagine that it can be any method with the above
signature
Can even thing of M as a reference variable that refers to a
method of the above signature
Obviously, w is a variable of type String.
What if we call M(w)?
We do not know whether it will return true, or false
It may not return at all (loops indeﬁnitely)
We have no idea what are M and w
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 17:
Language of a Method
So, here is what we can say about the method N1() below:
boolean N1(String x)
{
boolean p = M(w);
if(p)
return true;
else
return false;
}
If M(w) returns true, the language of N1() is Σ∗
If M(w) returns false, the language of N1() is ∅
If M(w) loops indeﬁnitely, the language of N1() is ∅
The behavior of the methods N1() depends on what happen
when M(w) is called
But again, we have no idea what are M() and w
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Turing Machines as Methods

Slide 18:
Language of a Method
Given a Turing machine M and a string w, the method N1()
is pretty much the same as the Turing machine below:
N1 = “On input x:
1
Run M on input w
2
If M accepts w, accept
If M rejects w, reject”
Similarly, we have no idea what is the language of N1 but at
least we know that
If M accepts w, L(N1) = Σ∗
If M rejects w, L(N1) = ∅
If M loops indeﬁnitely on w, L(N1) = ∅
We can also conclude that
If M accepts w, the language of N1 is not empty
If M does not accept w, the language of N1 is empty
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Reducibility 04

Slide 2:
Undecidability Proofs
So far, we have only one undecidable language:
ATM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM that accepts w}
This is a fact since we already prove it
Given a language A, how to prove that it is undecidable?
First, we are going to use the proof by contradiction
Assume that A is decidable
Show that since A is decidable, ATM is decidable
Contradict with the fact that ATM is undecidable
A is undecidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 04

Slide 3:
Halting Problem
From a programmer point of view:
Given a method foo and an input x, there is no algorithm that
can check whether foo will halt on input x
For some methods, you may be able to spot that it will halt or
will not halt but not all
But there are inﬁnite number of methods and inﬁnite number
of inputs
From the computation theory point of view:
Given a Turing machine M and input string w, there is no
algorithm that can check whether M will halt on input w
Formally,
HALT TM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM that halts on input w}
To prove halting problem, we just need to show that
HALT TM is undecidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 04

Slide 4:
HALT TM
First, assume that HALT TM is decidable
Next, we need to show that since HALT TM is decidable ATM
is decidable
We need to take the advantage of HALT TM being decidable
to help us decide ATM
What does it mean when HALT TM is decidable?
There exists a TM R that decides HALT TM
What TM R (a decider for HALT TM) can do?
If we run R on input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a
string, R will either accept or reject ⟨M, w⟩
If R accepts ⟨M, w⟩⇝⟨M, w⟩∈HALT TM
M halts on input w
If R rejects ⟨M, w⟩⇝⟨M, w⟩̸∈HALT TM
M does not halts on input w
Can R loops in deﬁnitely on input ⟨M, w⟩?
No because R is a decider
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 04

Slide 5:
HALT TM
When HALT TM is decidable, given a TM M′and a string w′,
we know whether M′halts on input w′
By running R on input ⟨M′, w′⟩
If R accepts ⟨M′, w′⟩⇝⟨M′, w′⟩∈HALT TM
M′halts on input w′
If R rejects ⟨M′, w′⟩⇝⟨M′, w′⟩̸∈HALT TM
M′does not halt on input w′
We need to use TM R to help us decide ATM
But what does it mean when ATM is undecidable?
Given a TM M and a string w, we have no idea whether
M accepts w or
M does not accepts w
If we can distinguish between M accepts w and M does not
accept w, ATM is decidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 04

Slide 6:
HALT TM
What if we can construct TM M′and w′(from the TM M
and the string w) such that
If M accepts w, M′halts on input w′
If M does not accept w, M′does not halt on input w′
and give ⟨M′, w′⟩to TM R?
By running R on the above input ⟨M′, w′⟩:
If R accepts ⟨M′, w′⟩:
R accepts ⟨M′, w′⟩⇝⟨M′, w′⟩∈HALT TM
⟨M′, w′⟩∈HALT TM ⇝M′halts on input w′
M′halts on input w′⇝M accepts w
M accepts w ⇝⟨M, w⟩∈ATM
If R rejects ⟨M′, w′⟩:
R rejects ⟨M′, w′⟩⇝⟨M′, w′⟩̸∈HALT TM
⟨M′, w′⟩̸∈HALT TM ⇝M′does not halt on input w′
M′does not halt on input w′⇝M does not accept w
M does not accept w ⇝⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM
This gives us the conclusion that ATM is decidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 04

Slide 7:
HALT TM
Again, we need to construct TM M′and w′(from the TM M
and the string w) such that
If M accepts w, M′halts on input w′
If M does not accept w, M′does not halt on input w′
Here is an example of TM M′:
M′= “On input x:
1
Run M on input w
2
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, enter inﬁnite loop.”
Note that there are inﬁnite number of TMs that satisfy the
above speciﬁcation
Let’s analyze the behavior of the above TM M′case-by-case
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 04

Slide 8:
HALT TM
The TM M′:
M′= “On input x:
1
Run M on input w
2
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, enter inﬁnite loop.”
What if M accepts w (⟨M, w⟩∈ATM)?
M accepts w ⇝M′accepts all input strings
M′accepts all input strings ⇝M′halts on all input strings
M′halts on all input strings ⇝M′halts on ε
M′halts on ε ⇝⟨M′, ε⟩∈HALT TM
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 9:
HALT TM
The TM M′:
M′= “On input x:
1
Run M on input w
2
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, enter inﬁnite loop.”
What if M rejects w (⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM)?
M rejects w ⇝M′enters inﬁnite loop on all input strings
M′enters inﬁnite loop on all input strings ⇝M′does not halt
on all input strings
M′does not halt on all input strings ⇝M′does not halt on ε
M′does not halt on ε ⇝⟨M′, ε⟩̸∈HALT TM
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 04

Slide 10:
HALT TM
The TM M′:
M′= “On input x:
1
Run M on input w
2
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, enter inﬁnite loop.”
What if M loops indeﬁnitely on w (⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM)?
M loops indeﬁnitely on w ⇝M′loops indeﬁnitely (in step 1)
on all input strings
M′loops indeﬁnitely on all input strings ⇝M′does not halt
on all input strings
M′does not halt on all input strings ⇝M′does not halt on ε
M′does not halt on ε ⇝⟨M′, ε⟩̸∈HALT TM

Slide 11:
HALT TM
Again, we just construct TM M′and w′= ε from a TM M
and a string w such that
If ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, ⟨M′, ε⟩∈HALT TM
If ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, ⟨M′, ε⟩̸∈HALT TM
Now, we can simply run TM R (a decider for HALT TM to
check whether ⟨M′, ε⟩is in HALT TM
If R accepts ⟨M′, ε⟩⇝⟨M′, ε⟩∈HALT TM ⇝⟨M, w⟩∈ATM
If R rejects ⟨M′, ε⟩⇝⟨M′, ε⟩̸∈HALT TM ⇝⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM
Let’s see the whole proof where we construct a TM S that
decides ATM where
S constructs TM M′as we discussed earlier and
uses R as a helper machine to help decide ATM
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 04

Slide 12:
HALT TM
Prove that HALT TM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM that halts in input w} is undecidable.
Proof: Assume that HALT TM is decidable. Since HALT TM is decidable, there exists
a TM R that decides HALT TM. Let construct a TM S (to decide ATM) as follows:
S = “On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct TM M′as follows:
M′= “On input x:
1
Run M on input w
2
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, enter inﬁnite loop.”
2
Run R on input ⟨M′, ε⟩
3
If R accepts ⟨M′, ε⟩, accept. If R rejects ⟨M′, ε⟩, reject.”
Next, we need to prove that TM S is a decider for ATM.
Prove that if ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩: Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM.
Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M is a TM that accepts w. Since M accepts w, from the
deﬁnition of TM M′, M′halts on all strings including ε. Since M′halts on ε,
⟨M′, ε⟩∈HALT TM. Since R is a decider for HALT TM, by running R on input
⟨M′, ε⟩, R will accept ⟨M′, ε⟩. Since R accepts ⟨M′, ε⟩, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩.
Prove that if ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, S rejects ⟨M, w⟩: Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM.
Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M is a TM that does not accept w. Since M does not
accept w, from the deﬁnition of TM M′, M′does not halt on all strings
including ε. Since M′does not halt on ε, ⟨M′, ε⟩̸∈HALT TM. Since R is a
decider for HALT TM, by running R on input ⟨M′, ε⟩, R will reject ⟨M′, ε⟩.
Since R rejects ⟨M′, ε⟩, S rejects ⟨M, w⟩.
This show that TM S is a decider for ATM. Therefore, ATM is decidable ⇝
contradiction. Therefore, HALT TM is undecidable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 04

Slide 13:
Undecidability
Let’s recap and make some notes
To prove that a language A is undecidable:
Assume that A is decidable
Since A is decidable, there exists a TM R that decides A
Next, we try to decide ATM using R as a helper machine
To decide ATM, we need to know whether ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM
In other words, we need to know whether M accepts w
Since R is a decider for the language A, R can tell you
whether a string s is in A
So, we need to construct a string s such that whether s is in
A depending on whether M accepts w
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 04

Slide 14:
Undecidability
To construct a string s such that whether s is in A depending
on whether M accepts w, there are two choices:
1
Choice #1:
If M accepts w, s ∈A
If M does not accept w, s ̸∈A
2
Choice #2:
If M accepts w, s ̸∈A
If M does not accept w, s ∈A
Which one?
It depends on the problem
For some problems, either choices will work
For some problems, only one of those choices will work
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 04

Slide 15:
Undecidability
From the previous example, we have two choices as well:
1
Choice #1:
If M accepts w, ⟨M′, w′⟩∈HALT TM
If M does not accept w, ⟨M′, w′⟩̸∈HALT TM
2
Choice #2:
If M accepts w, ⟨M′, w′⟩̸∈HALT TM
If M does not accept w, ⟨M′, w′⟩∈HALT TM
We picked the ﬁrst choice
If you try, you will see that you cannot construct a TM M′
that satisﬁes the second choice
The hard part is when M loops indeﬁnitely on input w
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 04

Slide 16:
ETM
Problem: Given a TM M, how to check whether L(M) = ∅?
Unfortunately, we cannot
Note that a TM may have a path from the start state to its
accept state but it accepts no string
Formally:
ETM = {⟨M⟩| M is a TM and L(M) = ∅}
To show that there is no algorithm that can solve the above
problem, we need to show that ETM is undecidable

Slide 19:
ETM
Here is an example of TM M′(constructed from a TM M
and a string w) that satisﬁes:
If M accepts w, L(M′) ̸= ∅
If M does not accept w, L(M′) = ∅
TM M′:
M′= “On input x:
1
Run M on input w
2
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, reject.”
From the above TM M′:
If M accepts w, M′accepts all strings (L(M′) ̸= ∅)
If M does not accepts w:
If M rejects w, M′rejects all strings (L(M′) = ∅)
If M loops indeﬁnitely on input w, M′loops indeﬁnitely on all
strings (L(M′) = ∅)
Now, let’s see the whole proof
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 20:
ETM
Prove that ETM = {⟨M⟩| M is a TM and L(M) = ∅} is undecidable.
Proof: Assume that ETM is decidable. Since ETM is decidable, there exists a TM R
that decides ETM. Let construct a TM S (to decide ATM) as follows:
S = “On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct TM M′as follows:
M′= “On input x:
1
Run M on input w
2
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, reject.”
2
Run R on input ⟨M′⟩
3
If R accepts ⟨M′⟩, reject. If R rejects ⟨M′⟩, accept.”
Next, we need to prove that TM S is a decider for ATM.
Prove that if ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩: Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM.
Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M is a TM that accepts w. Since M accepts w, from the
deﬁnition of TM M′, M′accepts all strings. In other words, L(M′) ̸= ∅. Since
L(M′) ̸= ∅, ⟨M′⟩̸∈ETM. Since R is a decider for ETM, by running R on
input ⟨M′⟩, R will reject ⟨M′⟩. Since R rejects ⟨M′⟩, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩.
Prove that if ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, S rejects ⟨M, w⟩: Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM.
Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M is a TM that does not accept w. Since M does not
accept w, from the deﬁnition of TM M′, M′accepts no strings. In other words,
L(M′) = ∅. Since L(M′) = ∅, ⟨M′⟩∈ETM. Since R is a decider for ETM, by
running R on input ⟨M′⟩, R will accept ⟨M′⟩. Since R accepts ⟨M′⟩, S rejects
⟨M, w⟩.
This show that TM S is a decider for ATM. Therefore, ATM is decidable ⇝
contradiction. Therefore, ETM is undecidable.

REGULARTM is Undecidable
Problem: Is there an algorithm to determine whether the
language of a TM is regular?
Formally,
REGULARTM = {⟨M⟩| M is a TM and L(M) is a regular language}
Unfortunately, no algorithm can solve this problem
To verify this, we need to prove that REGULARTM is
undecidable
As usual, we are going to use the proof by contradiction
Assume that REGULARTM is decidable
Thus, there exists a TM R that decides REGULARTM
This gives us the ability to check whether the language of a
TM M′is regular or not regular
As usual, we want to show that if REGULARTM is decidable,
ATM is decidable
So, we need to construct a TM M′such that whether the
language of TM M′is regular or not depending on whether M
accepts w

2
Choice #2:
If M accepts w, L(M′) is not regular

Choice #1
If M accepts w, L(M′) is regular
If M does not accept w, L(M′) is not regular
Here is an example of a TM M′that satisﬁes choice #1:
M′= “On input x:
1
If x is in the form of 0n1n, accept.
2
If x is not in the form of 0n1n, run M on input w.
3
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, reject.”
From the deﬁnition of the above TM M′:
If M accepts w, L(M′) = Σ∗
If M does not accept w:
If M rejects w, L(M′) = {0n1n| n ≥0}
If M loops indeﬁnitely on w, L(M′) = {0n1n| n ≥0}

Slide 6:
REGULARTM is undecidable (Choice #1)
Proof: Assume that REGULARTM is decidable. Since REGULARTM is decidable,
there exists a TM R that decides REGULARTM. Let construct a TM S as follows:
S = “On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct TM M′as follows:
M′= “On input x:
1
If x is in the form of 0n1n, accept.
2
If x is not in the form of 0n1n, run M on input w.
3
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, reject.”
2
Run R on input ⟨M′⟩.
3
If R accepts ⟨M′⟩, accept. If R rejects ⟨M′⟩, reject.”
Next, we need to prove that TM S is a decider for ATM.
Prove that if ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩: Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM.
Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M is a TM that accepts w. Since M accepts w, from the
deﬁnition of TM M′, L(M′) is Σ∗which is a regular language. Since L(M′) is
regular, ⟨M′⟩∈REGULARTM. Since R is a decider for REGULARTM, by
running R on input ⟨M′⟩, R will accept ⟨M′⟩. Since R accepts ⟨M′⟩, S accepts
⟨M, w⟩.
Prove that if ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, S rejects ⟨M, w⟩: Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM.
Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M is a TM that does not accept w. Since M does not
accept w, from the deﬁnition of TM M′, L(M′) is {0n1n| n ≥0} which is not
a regular language. Since L(M′) is not regular, ⟨M′⟩̸∈REGULARTM. Since
R is a decider for REGULARTM, by running R on input ⟨M′⟩, R will reject
⟨M′⟩. Since R rejects ⟨M′⟩, S rejects ⟨M, w⟩.
This show that TM S is a decider for ATM. Therefore, ATM is decidable ⇝
contradiction. Therefore, REGULARTM is undecidable.

Slide 7:
More Fact for Contradiction
So far, we have been using ATM as the mean of contradiction
Because we know from the fact that ATM is undecidable
But now, we have the following undecidable languages:
ATM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM that accepts w}
HALT TM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM that halts on input w}
ETM = {⟨M⟩| M is a TM and L(M) = ∅}
REGULARTM = {⟨M⟩| M is a TM and L(M) is regular}
We can now use those undecidable languages
For example, to show that a language A is undecidable, we can
show that if A is decidable, ETM is decidable
But we can always use ATM
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

EQTM is Undecidable
Problem: Is there an algorithm to determine whether
languages of two TMs M1 and M2 are identical?
Formally,
EQTM = {⟨M1, M2⟩| M1 and M2 are TMs and L(M1) = L(M2)}
Unfortunately, EQTM is undecidable
As usual, we are going to assume that EQTM is decidable
In other words, given two TMs M1 and M2, we know whether
⟨M1, M2⟩∈EQTM or not
For this language, we generally need to construct two TMs
M1 and M2 from a TM M and a string w
For this problem, we are going to look at two proofs:
Contradict with the fact that ATM is undecidable
Contradict with the fact that ETM is undecidable

Slide 9:
EQTM is Undecidable
Prove that EQTM = {⟨M1, M2⟩| M1 and M2 are TMs and L(M1) = L(M2)} is
undecidable.
Proof: Assume that EQTM is decidable. Since EQTM is decidable, there exists a TM
R that decides EQTM. Let construct a TM S (to decide ATM) as follows:
S = “On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct TM M1 as follows:
M1 = “On input x:
1
accept.
2
Construct TM M2 as follows:
M2 = “On input x:
1
Run M on input w.
2
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, reject.”
3
Run R on input ⟨M1, M2⟩.
4
If R accepts ⟨M1, M2⟩, accept. If R rejects ⟨M1, M2⟩, reject.”
Next, we need to prove that TM S is a decider for ATM.
Prove that if ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩:
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M is a TM that accepts w.
Since M accepts w, from the deﬁnition of TM M1, L(M1) = Σ∗and from the
deﬁnition of TM M2, L(M2) = Σ∗. In other words, L(M1) = L(M2). Since
L(M1) = L(M2), ⟨M1, M2⟩∈EQTM. Since R is a decider for EQTM, by
running R on input ⟨M1, M2⟩, R will accept ⟨M1, M2⟩. Since R accepts
⟨M1, M2⟩, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩.

Proof: (continue)
Prove that if ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, S rejects ⟨M, w⟩:
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M is a TM that does not
accept w. Since M does not accept w, from the deﬁnition of TM M1,
L(M1) = Σ∗and from the deﬁnition of TM M2, L(M2) = ∅. In other words,
L(M1) ̸= L(M2). Since L(M1) ̸= L(M2), ⟨M1, M2⟩̸∈EQTM. Since R is a
decider for EQTM, by running R on input ⟨M1, M2⟩, R will reject ⟨M1, M2⟩.
Since R rejects ⟨M1, M2⟩, S rejects ⟨M, w⟩.
This show that TM S is a decider for ATM. Therefore, ATM is decidable ⇝
contradiction. Therefore, EQTM is undecidable.

Conclusion
So far, to prove that a language A is undecidable, we use
proof by contradiction
Assume that A is decidable
Since A is decidable, there exists a TM R that decides A
Then we show that if A is decidable, ATM is decidable
Another known undecidable language can be used instead of
ATM
So, we need to construct a TM S that decides ATM and prove
that S is a decider for ATM
Given a TM M and a string w, TM S need to construct a
string s such that whether s ∈A depends on whether M
accepts w
Then use TM R (a decider for A) as a helper machine to
decide ATM
Try to prove that ONE TM is undecidable using the choice #2.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 2:
Reductions
Recall the concept of reducibility:
A problem A is reducible to a problem B if the solution to the
problem B can be used to solve the problem A
To show that a language A is undecidable, we show that ATM
(or other undecidable language) is reducible to A
ATM is reducible to HALT TM
We want to know whether ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM
Turn ⟨M, w⟩to ⟨M′, w′⟩(in our case w′= ε)
Check whether ⟨M′, w′⟩∈HALT TM
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔⟨M′, w′⟩∈HALT TM
ATM is reducible to ETM
We want to know whether ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM
Turn ⟨M, w⟩to ⟨M′⟩
Check whether ⟨M′⟩∈ETM
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔⟨M′⟩̸∈ETM
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 5:
Deﬁnitions
Recall deﬁnitions about conﬁguration:
Suppose the start state of TM M is q0, the start
conﬁguration of M on input w is q0w
An accepting conﬁguration is a conﬁguration uqacceptv for
strings u and v
A rejecting conﬁguration is a conﬁguration uqrejectv for
strings u and v
A conﬁguration Ci legally go to Cj according to the
transitions of the TM M
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 06

Slide 6:
Reductions via Computation Histories
Deﬁnition 5.5
Let M be a Turing machine and w an input string. An accepting
computation history for M on w is a sequence of conﬁgurations,
C1, C2, . . . Cl where C1 is the start conﬁguration of M on w, Cl is
an accepting conﬁguration of M, and each Ci legally follows from
Ci−1 according to the rules of M. A rejecting computation
history for M on w is deﬁned similarly, except that Cl is a
rejecting conﬁguration.
Note that M must halt on input w. Otherwise, accepting or
rejection computation history does not exists
For simplicity, we only consider deterministic Turing machine
If M halts on w, exactly one computation history exists
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 7:
Reductions via Computation Histories
Again, to show that a language A is undecidable, we show
that ATM is reducible to A
Given a TM M and a string w, there is no algorithm to check
whether ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM because ATM is undecidable
But we know that
If M accepts w, ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM
If M does not accept w, ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM
Using the concept of computation histories:
If the accepting computation history of M on input w exists
⇝M accepts w ⇝⟨M, w⟩∈ATM
If the accepting computation history of M on input w does
not exists ⇝M does not accept w ⇝⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM
Let’s try to use reduction via computation histories with a
variation of a TM called Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA)

Deﬁnition 5.6
A linear bounded automaton is a restricted type of Turing
machine wherein the tape head isn’t permitted to move oﬀthe
portion of the tape containing the input. If the machine tries to
move its head oﬀeither end of the input, the head stays where it is.

Slide 9:
Linear Bounded Automaton
Problem: Given an LBA M and a string w, is there an
algorithm to determine whether M accepts w?
Formally,
ALBA = {⟨M, w⟩| M is an LBA that accepts w}
Is ALBA decidable?
This sounds like ATM
ATM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM that accepts w}
Even though ATM is undecidable, ALBA is actually decidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 10:
Linear Bounded Automaton
Recall a conﬁguration uqv
uv is the content of the tape
Recall that very TM has an inﬁnite long tape
Therefore, there are inﬁnite number of distinct strings uv
But the tape of an LBA is ﬁnite
The length of its tape on input string w is |w|
The number of states and |Γ| of an LBA are also ﬁnite
Because of these, the set of all distinct conﬁgurations that an
LBA can have on an input string w is ﬁnite
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 11:
Linear Bounded Automaton
Consider an LBA with the following speciﬁcation:
The number of states is q
|Γ| = g (number of symbols in its tape alphabets)
The length of the tape is n
From the above LBA:
For each square on the tape, there can be one of g symbols
There are the total of gnunique tape contents
There are n possible positions of the tape head
For each tape head position, it can be in one of q states
Thus, there are exactly qngndistinct conﬁgurations
Because of this, we can detect when an LBA is entering a
loop by checking whether sequence of conﬁgurations when
running an LBA and a string w contains a duplicate
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 06

Slide 12:
Linear Bounded Automaton
To detect an inﬁnite loop in an LBA, consider an LBA M
where its series of conﬁguration while processing w is as
follows:
C1, C2, C3, C4, C5, C6, C7, C8, C9, C10, C11, . . .
What if the conﬁguration C3 is the same as C9?
The content of the tape are exactly the same
The current state of M are the same
The tape head are at the same location
Thus
C10 will be the same as C4
C11 will be the same as C5
and so on
However, this method requires us to keep track of sequence of
conﬁgurations
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 13:
Linear Bounded Automaton
Recall that if we run an LBA on input w for 1 step, we
already sees two conﬁgurations
The start conﬁguration
The next conﬁguration that legally goes from the start
conﬁguration
Thus, if we run an LBA on input w for k steps, we already see
k + 1 conﬁgurations
Recall that the total number of distinct conﬁgurations is qngn,
we can simply run the LBA for qngnsteps or until it halts
If it does not halt after qngnsteps, we already see qngn+ 1
conﬁgurations
But there are only qngndistinct conﬁgurations
Thus, at least two of them must be the same (loop indeﬁnitely)
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Linear Bounded Automaton
Theorem 5.9
ALBA = {⟨M, w⟩| M is an LBA that accepts string w} is
decidable.
Construct a TM L that decides ALBA as follows:
L = “On input ⟨M, w⟩, where M is an LBA and w is a string:
1
Simulate M on input w for qngnsteps or until it halts.
2
If M has halted, accept if it has accepted and reject if it has
rejected. if it has not halted, reject.”

ELBA is Undecidable
Now, we just verify that ALBA is decidable
But what about the problem of determining whether an LBA
M accepts no strings (L(M) = ∅)
Formally,
ELBA = {⟨M⟩| M is an LBA and L(M) = ∅}
It turns out that this problem is unsolvable
In other words, ELBA is undecidable
Let’s try to prove it using the same method that we used to
prove that ETM is undecidable to try to prove that ELBA is
undecidable

ELBA is Undecidable
Assume that ELBA. Since ELBA is decidable, there exists a TM R that decides
ELBA. Construct a TM S (to decide ATM) as follows:
S = “On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct an LBA M′as follows:
M′= “On input x:
1
Run M on input w.
2
If M accepts w, accept; otherwise, reject.”
2
Run R (a decider for ELBA) on input ⟨M′⟩.
3
If R accepts ⟨M′⟩, reject. If R rejects ⟨M′⟩, accept.”
Next, we need to prove that the TM S above, decides ATM. . .
Did you see a problem in the above prove?
M′(constructed by S) must be an LBA
An LBA has a ﬁnite long tape
To run TM M on input w, the LBA M′may need inﬁnite
long tape
You cannot construct an LBA that can always run a TM M
on an input w

Slide 17:
ELBA is Undecidable
In this case, we need to show that ATM is reducible to ELBA
using the computation history method
This method will also give you the same conclusion:
If ELBA is decidable, ATM is decidable
The idea is to construct an LBA B that accepts exactly one
string
The string will be a string representation of the accepting
computation history for M on w
Why this work?
If ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ⇝M accepts w ⇝the accepting
computation history for M on w exists ⇝LBA B accepts
exactly one string ⇝L(B) ̸= ∅⇝⟨B⟩̸∈ELBA
If ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM ⇝M does not accept w ⇝the accepting
computation history for M on w does not exists ⇝LBA B
accepts no strings ⇝L(B) = ∅⇝⟨B⟩∈ELBA
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

ELBA is Undecidable
But can we construct an LBA that accepts exactly one string
which is the accepting computation history for M on w
without requiring an inﬁnite amount of tape?
Recall that an LBA cannot always runs M on input w because
of its ﬁnite long tape
So, can an LBA checks whether a given string is the accepting
computation history for M on w without actually run M on
input w?
It turns out, we can

With an input string x to the LBA B, the machine B must

x is in the form of #C1#C2# . . . #Cl# for a number l
2

Given the deﬁnition of a TM M and a input string w:

Slide 21:
ELBA is Undecidable
Theorem 5.10
ELBA = {⟨M⟩| M is an LBA and L(M) = ∅} is undecidable.
For the sake of contradiction, assume that ELBA is decidable
There exists a TM R decides ELBA
Construct a TM S decides ATM as follows:
S = “On input ⟨M, w⟩, where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct LBA B from M and w as described in the proof idea
2
Run R on input ⟨B⟩
3
If R rejects ⟨B⟩, accept; if R accepts ⟨B⟩, reject.”
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 06

Slide 22:
ELBA is Undecidable
Sketch of the proofs:
If ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM:
⇝M accepts w
⇝The accepting computation history for M on w exists
⇝B accepts exactly one string
⇝L(B) ̸= ∅
⇝R rejects ⟨B⟩
⇝S accepts ⟨M, w⟩
If ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM:
⇝M does not accept w
⇝The accepting computation history for M on w does not exists
⇝B accepts no strings
⇝L(B) = ∅
⇝R accepts ⟨B⟩
⇝S rejects ⟨M, w⟩
This show that S is a decider for ATM
In other words, ATM is decidable — contradiction
Therefore, ELBA is undecidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

To prove that EQTM is undecidable, we need to show that

1
Create a computable function f in the form of a TM F
This TM F takes ⟨M, w⟩(an instance of ATM) and construct
two TMs M1 and M2
L(M1) = L(M2) if M accepts w
L(M1) ̸= L(M2) if M does not accepts w (either rejects or

Output ⟨M1, M2⟩(an instance of EQTM)

Prove that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔⟨M1, M2⟩∈EQTM

new LBA M′

Common Theme
All three methods so far share a common process
Take ⟨M, w⟩(an instance of ATM)
Construct a new TM, TMs, or LBA
Behaviors of new TM, TMs, or LBA depend on whether M
accepts w
Construct TM or TMs from TM M and string w are quite
straightforward
We can simply run M on input w
Construct LBA from TM M and string w is not quite
straightforward
We cannot simply run M on input w
Use computation history (sequence of conﬁgurations)

Slide 6:
Post Correspondence Problem (PCP)
A Post Correspondence Problem (PCP) is a problem
concerning string manipulation.
Consider a domino
 a
ab

There are two non-empty strings in each domino, one on the
top and one on the bottom.
Given a set of dominoes
( b
ca

,
 a
ab

,
ca
a

,
abc
c
)
Is it possible to make a horizontal line of one or more
dominoes, with duplicates allowed, so that the string obtained
by reading across the top halves matches the one obtained by
reading across the bottom?

Slide 8:
Post Correspondence Problem (PCP)
An instance P of PCP is a collection of dominoes
P =
(t1
b1

,

, . . . ,
tk

A match is a sequence i1, i2, . . . , il such that
ti1ti2 . . . til = bi1bi2 . . . bil
As usual, we deﬁne PCP as a set
PCP = {⟨P⟩| P is an instance of the Post Correspondence
Problem with a match}.
Is PCP decidable?
Given an instance P (a set of dominoes) of PCP, can we
decide whether P ∈PCP or not?
P ∈PCP if P has a match
P ̸∈PCP if P does not have a match

input:
Slide 9:
Post Correspondence Problem (PCP)
To prove that PCP is undecidable, we use the same method
as what we did for other undecidable languages
1
Assume that PCP is decidable and obtain its decider R
2
Construct TM S with the help of R to decide ATM
Take ⟨M, w⟩(an instance of ATM) and construct P an
instance of PCP which is a set of dominos
P has a match if M accepts w
P does not have a match if M does not accept w
3
Prove that S is a decider for ATM
4
Conclude that ATM is decidable — contradiction
5
Therefore, PCP is undecidable
Note that we have to construct a set of dominoes from TM
M and string w
This process is long but straightforward
We also need to look at another closely related problem
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Post Correspondence Problem

Slide 10:
Modiﬁed Post Correspondence Problem (MPCP)
A Modiﬁed Post Correspondence Problem (MPCP) looks
exactly like PCP but the match is required to start with the
ﬁrst domino.
An instance P of MPCP is a collection of dominoes
P =
(t1
b1

,
t2
b2

, . . . ,
tk
bk

,
)
A match is a sequence 1,i2, i3, . . . , il such that
t1ti2 . . . til = b1bi2 . . . bil
As usual, we deﬁne MPCP as a language
MPCP = {⟨P⟩| P is an instance of the Post Correspondence
Problem with a match that starts with he ﬁrst
domino}.
We are going to construct P′(an instance of MPCP) from
TM M and string w such that P′has a match if an only if M
accepts w

Slide 13:
Computation History
Recall that M accepts w if and only if accepting
computation history for M on input w exists
Since M (from previous slide) accepts 00, the accepting
computation history for M on input 00 exists
q000 →0q10 →00qaccept
We can turn it into a string as
#q000#0q10#00qaccept#
This will be our match
If the accepting computation history for M on input w exist,
there is a match
And the match looks exactly like the computation history
If the accepting computation history for M on input w does
not exist, there is no match
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

to an instance of PCP.

a speciﬁc domino only.

Slide 46:
Conclusion
Assume that PCP is decidable. Thus, there exists TM R
decides PCP.
Construct TM S that decides ATM
S =“On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct a set of dominoes P an instance of PCP from TM
M and input w as we discussed
2
Then run R on input ⟨P⟩
3
If R accepts ⟨P⟩, accept
If R rejects ⟨P⟩, reject
To prove that TM S a decider for ATM is straightforward

input:
Slide 47:
Conclusion
Show that TM S is a decider for ATM
1
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M accepts
w. Since M accepts w, the set of dominoes P constructed in
step 1 will have a match. In other words, ⟨P⟩∈PCP. Since R
is a decider for PCP, by running R on input ⟨P⟩, R will accept
⟨P⟩. Since R accepts ⟨P⟩, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩.
2
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M does
not accept w. Since M does not accept w, the set of
dominoes P constructed in step 1 will have not have match. In
other words, ⟨P⟩̸∈PCP. Since R is a decider for PCP, by
running R on input ⟨P⟩, R will reject ⟨P⟩. Since R rejects
⟨P⟩, S rejects ⟨M, w⟩.
This shows that S is a decider for ATM. Therefore, ATM is
decidable — contradiction
PCP is undecidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Reducibility 08

Slide 2:
Mapping Reducibility
Deﬁnition 5.20
Language A is mapping reducible to language B, written
A ≤m B, if there is a computable function f : Σ∗→Σ∗, where for
every w,
w ∈A ↔f(w) ∈B.

Slide 3:
Reductions
Crucial Features in a Reduction:
1
For every instance w of the ﬁrst problem that we start with,
we must be able to obtain the instance f(w) of the second
problem algorithmically
In our discussion, this reduction f : Σ∗→Σ∗will be a TM
that produces an output string
2
The answer to the second question for the instance f(w) must
be the same as the answer to the original question for w
w ∈A ↔f(w) ∈B.
In other words, for every w,
If f(w) ∈B, w must be in A
If f(w) ̸∈B, w must not be in A
3
The reduction does not have to be one-to-one or onto as long
as it satisﬁes the above property
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 4:
Mapping Reducibility
Theorem 5.22
If A ≤m B and B is decidable, then A is decidable.
Proof:
Assume that A ≤m B and B is decidable
Since A ≤m B, there exists a reduction in the form of a TM
F satisfying
w ∈A ↔F(w) ∈B
Since B is decidable, there exists a TM MB that decides B
We just need to use the same method as in Chapter 4 to show
that A is decidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 5:
Mapping Reducibility
Construct a decider N (to decide A) as follows:
N =“On input w:
1
Run TM F (a reduction from A to B) on w to produce F(w).
2
Run MB (a decider for B) on F(w)
3
If MB accepts F(w), accept. If MB rejects F(w), reject.”
Prove that if w ∈A, N accepts w:
Assume that w ∈A. Since w ∈A, from the property of the reduction F,
F(w) ∈B. Since F(w) ∈B and MB is a decider for B, by running MB on
input F(w), MB will accept F(w). Since MB accepts F(w), N accepts w.
Prove that if w ̸∈A, N rejects w:
Assume that w ̸∈A. Since w ̸∈A, from the property of the reduction F,
F(w) ̸∈B. Since F(w) ̸∈B and MB is a decider for B, by running MB on
input F(w), MB will reject F(w). Since MB rejects F(w), N rejects w.
This shows that N is a decider for A
Therefore A is decidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 08

Slide 6:
Mapping Reducibility
Theorem 5.22
If A ≤m B and B is decidable, then A is decidable.
Let the following:
P ≡A ≤m B
Q ≡B is decidable
R ≡A is decidable
Since (P ∧Q) →R ≡(P ∧¬R) →¬Q, we have
Corollary 5.23
If A ≤m B and A is undecidable, then B is undecidable.

Slide 7:
Mapping Reducibility
Corollary 5.23
If A ≤m B and A is undecidable, then B is undecidable.
We already know from the fact that ATM is undecidable
Because of the above Corollary, to show that a language B is
undecidable, we just need to show that ATM ≤m B
ATM can be replaced by other undecidable language such as
ETM
To show that ATM ≤m B, we need to construct a TM F that
behaves like a reduction from ATM to B satisfying
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈B
Also need to prove that F actually satisﬁes the above property

Slide 8:
HALT TM is undecidable
ATM and HALTTM
ATM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM and M accepts w}
HALT TM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM and M halts on input w}
To use mapping reducibility to show that HALT TM is
undecidable:
We need to show that ATM is mapping reducible to HALT TM
(ATM ≤m HALT TM)
Since ATM ≤m HALT TM and ATM is undecidable, from
Corollary 5.23, HALT TM is undecidable
To show that ATM ≤m HALT TM, we need a computable
function F : Σ∗→Σ∗(TM F) such that
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈HALT TM
Note that F(⟨M, w⟩) must be in the form of ⟨M′, w′⟩(the
format of HALT TM)

HALT TM is undecidable
A computable function F where F(⟨M, w⟩) = ⟨M′, w′⟩is a
TM
A TM that takes ⟨M, w⟩as its input on its tape and produces
the output ⟨M′, w′⟩on its tape after it is halted
Note that TM F must be a reduction from ATM to HALT TM:
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈HALT TM
where F(⟨M, w⟩) = ⟨M′, w′⟩
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM →F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈HALT TM
⇝M accepts w →M′halts on input w′
⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM →F(⟨M, w⟩) ̸∈HALT TM
⇝M does not accept w →M′does not halt on input w′

HALT TM is Undecidable
Machine F computes a reduction f:
F =“On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct the following machine M′:
M′=“On input x:
1
Run M on w.
2
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, enter inﬁnite loop.”
2
Output ⟨M′, ε⟩.”
Next, we need to check whether TM F satisﬁes
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔⟨M′, w′⟩∈HALT TM
where ⟨M′, ε⟩= F(⟨M, w⟩) (the output of TM F)

Slide 11:
HALT TM is Undecidable
Show that
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈HALT TM
where F(⟨M, w⟩) = ⟨M′, ε⟩
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M is a
TM that accepts w. Since M accepts w, from the deﬁnition of
TM M′, M′halts (accepts) on all strings including ε. Since
M′halts on ε, ⟨M′, ε⟩∈HALT TM. Since
⟨M′, ε⟩= F(⟨M, w⟩), F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈HALT TM.
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M is a
TM that does not accept w. Since M does not accept w, from
the deﬁnition of TM M′, M′does not halt on all strings
including ε. Since M′does not halt on ε, ⟨M′, ε⟩̸∈HALT TM.
Since ⟨M′, ε⟩= F(⟨M, w⟩), F(⟨M, w⟩) ̸∈HALT TM.
This show that TM F is a reduction from ATM to HALT TM
In other words, ATM ≤m HALT TM
Since ATM is undecidable, HALT TM is undecidable (Corollary
5.23)
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

1
Run M on input w.
2
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, enter inﬁnite loop.”

If R accepts ⟨M′, ε⟩, accept. If R rejects ⟨M′, ε⟩, reject.”
Method #2: Construct a reduction F from ATM to

M′= “On input x:

Run M on input w.

Slide 15:
REGULARTM is Undecidable
Use the mapping reducibility to show that
REGULARTM = {⟨M⟩| M is a TM and L(M) is regular} is undecidable.
Solution: We need to construct a reduction F from ATM to REGULARTM satisfying
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈REGULARTM
Construct a TM F as follows:
F = “On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct a TM M′as follows:
M′= “On input x:
1
If x is in the form of 0n1n, accept.
2
If x is not in the form of 0n1n, run M on input w. If M accepts w,
accept; otherwise, reject.”
2
Output ⟨M′⟩.”
Next we need to show that the above TM F satisﬁes the property:
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔⟨M′⟩∈REGULARTM
where ⟨M′⟩= F(⟨M, w⟩) (output of TM F):
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 08

Slide 16:
REGULARTM is Undecidable
Continue:
If ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈REGULARTM:
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M is a TM that accepts w.
Since M accepts w, from the deﬁnition of TM M′, L(M′) = Σ∗. Since
L(M′) = Σ∗, L(M′) is regular. Since L(M′) is regular,
⟨M′⟩∈REGULARTM. Since F(⟨M, w⟩) = ⟨M′⟩,
F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈REGULARTM.
If ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, F(⟨M, w⟩) ̸∈REGULARTM:
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M is a TM that does not
accept w. Since M does not accept w, from the deﬁnition of TM M′,
L(M′) = {0n1n| n ≥0}. Since L(M′) = {0n1n| n ≥0}, L(M′) is not
regular. Since L(M′) is not regular, ⟨M′⟩̸∈REGULARTM. Since
F(⟨M, w⟩) = ⟨M′⟩, F(⟨M, w⟩) ̸∈REGULARTM.
This show that TM F is a reduction from ATM to REGULARTM. Therefore
ATM ≤m REGULARTM. Since ATM is undecidable, by Corollary 5.23,
REGULARTM is undecidable.

Time Complexity 01

Slide 2:
Measuring Complexity
A solvable problem takes time to solve
Some problems may take too long to solve
We are going to measure the time to solve a problem by an
algorithm
An algorithm will be represented by a TM
Count the maximum number of steps to process an input of
length n until it halts
Let M be a deterministic Turing machine that halts on all
inputs
The running time or time complexity of M is the function
f : N →N, where f(n) is the maximum number of steps that
M uses on any input of length n
If f(n) is the running time of M, M runs in time f(n)
M is an f(n) time Turing machine
Customarily we use n to represent the length of the input
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 01

Slide 3:
Big-O and Small-o Notations
Given a TM M, its running time f(n) may look like the
following:
f(n) = 3n3+ 20n2+ 15n + 12
To eliminate the complexity of a running time of a Turing
machine, we estimate it
This is called asymptotic analysis
Consider only when the Turing machine runs on large input
Consider only the highest order term
Discard the coeﬃcient
We generally use Big-O and Small-o notations
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 01

Slide 4:
Big-O Deﬁnition
Let f and g be functions f, g : N →R+
We say that f(n) = O(g(n)) or f(n) is O(g(n)) if positive
integers c and n0 exist such that for every integer n ≥n0,
f(n) ≤cg(n)
When f(n) = O(g(n)):
g(n) is an upper bound for f(n), or
More precisely, g(n) is an asymptotic upper bound for f(n)
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 01

Show that f(n) = 6n3+ 2n2+ 20n + 45 is O(n3)

We know the following:
2n2≤2n3for n ≥1
20n ≤20n3for n ≥1
45 ≤45n3for n ≥1
Thus, we have
f(n) = 6n3+ 2n2+ 20n + 45 ≤6n3+ 2n3+ 20n3+ 45n3
= (6 + 2 + 20 + 45)n3
= 73n3for n ≥1
In other words,
f(n) ≤73n3
for n ≥1
From the above equation, what are c and n0?
c = 73
n0 = 1
Thus, 6n3+ 2n2+ 20 + 45 is O(n3)

Consider a Turing machine M1 that decides

1 Scan across the tape and reject if a 0 is found to the right of
a 1
This step requires TM M1 to move its tape head all the way

This step requires either n or 2n steps

2 Repeat if both 0s and 1s remain on the tape:
3
Scan across the tape, crossing oﬀa single 0 and a single 1.
Step 3 requires n steps or O(n)
Cross the ﬁrst 0, move the tape head n/2 step to the ﬁrst 1,
cross it oﬀand move the tape head n/2 step to position on
top of the ﬁrst of the remaining 0s
There will be n/2 0s and n/2 1s
The step 3 is repeated n/2 times
Steps 2 and 3 together is n/2 × O(n) or O(n2).

Simply scan the whole tape looking for a 0 or a 1
This step can be done in n step

Consider TM M2 that decides A = {0k1k| k ≥0}:
M2 = “On input string w:

Scan across the tape and reject if a 0 is found to the right of a
1.

3
Scan across the tape, checking whether the total number of

4
Scan again across the tape, crossing oﬀevery other 0

5
If no 0s and no 1s remain on the tape, accept. Otherwise,
reject.

Asymptotically Faster for Deciding A
Step 1 and 5 are O(n) as in M1
Step 3, to scan and check whether the number of 0s and 1s
are even or odd is O(n)
In step 4, to cross oﬀevery other 0s and 1s is O(n)
Every time this step is executed, the number of 0s and 1s are
cut in half
If there are n/2 0s and n/2 1s, step 4 needs to be repeated
1 + log2(n/2) times
For example (n = 16), 8 →4 →2 →1 →0 (cut in half 4
times) for input 0818
Steps 2, 3 and 4 all together is
(1 + log2(n/2)) × (O(n) + O(n)) = O(n log n)
Thus, TM M2 run-time is
O(n) + O(n log n) + O(n) = O(n log n)

Deciding A in Linear Time
The language A can be decided in O(n)
But we need the second tape
Two-tape TM M3 decides A in O(n)
M3 =“On input string w:
1
Scan across tape 1 and reject if a 0 is found to the right of a 1.
2
Scan across the 0s on tape 1 until the ﬁrst 1. At the same
time, copy the 0s onto tape 2.
3
Scan across the 1s on tape 1 until the end of the input. For
each 1 read on tape 1, cross oﬀa 0 on tape 2. If all 0s are
crossed oﬀbefore all the 1s are read, reject.
4
If all the 0s have now been crossed oﬀ, accept. If any 0s
remain, reject.”
Run-time of each step is O(n)
TM M3 runs in O(n)

Proof of Theorem 7.8
Let M be a k-tape TM for some k ≥2 that runs in t(n) on
input string of length n
We are going to construct a single-tape TM S that simulate
multitape TM M
The ﬁrst step is for S to copy contents of all M’s k tapes into
its tape
Note that contents of all M’s k tapes are ﬁxed except the ﬁrst
one (input tape)
Suppose the length of strings on tape 2 to tape k be a
constant c and the length of input string is n
It requires S to perform c + n + (k + 1) steps to copy
The k + 1 steps are from writing # symbols
Since c and k are constant, this step is O(n)
The next step is to simulate M’s steps
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 20:
Proof of Theorem 7.8
For each step of M, ﬁrst S needs to know symbols under M’s
tape heads
S needs to scan and remember all k-dot symbols
Since M performs t(n) steps, the maximum length of an
active portion of each tape is t(n)
If the length of the content of a tape is t(n) + 1, it requires
TM M t(n) step to reach the last symbol
By the time it gets to the last symbol, TM M halts
S needs k × t(n) steps to scan for all k-dot symbols
Scanning for all dot symbols is O(t(n))

Use breadth-ﬁrst search to achieve polynomial time for PATH:

Relatively Prime
Two numbers are relatively prime if 1 is the largest integer
that evenly divides them both
Formally,
RELPRIME = {⟨x, y⟩| x and y are relatively prime}
Is RELPRIME ∈P?
Brute-force algorithm:
Find all possible divisors of both numbers and accept if none
are greater than 1
If a number is represented by n-bit binary, the number of
possible value is 2n

(exponential)

Euclidean Algorithm for Greatest Common Divisor
We can check whether x and y are relatively prime by
computing their Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)
If gcd(x, y) = 1, x and y are relatively prime
Computing a gcd can be done using Euclidean Algorithm in a
form of a TM as follows:
E = “On input ⟨x, y⟩, where x and y are natural numbers:
1
Repeat until y = 0:
2
Assign x ←x mod y.
3
Exchange x and y.
4
Output x.”
When we run TM E on input ⟨x, y⟩where x and y are natural
numbers, it simply output their GCD

2
